one_line_takeaway: Local, per-agent LLM alignment cannot catch violations that only become visible when siloed departmental context is combined; the paper's own violation study shows all 8 tested frontier LLMs leak 14-98% of the time under fragmented context alone, and its proposed sidecar/taint-token architecture (Distributed Sentinel) cuts that to a 3-6% residual (F1=0.95 on their own 200-scenario benchmark) by exchanging only boolean cross-domain policy answers rather than raw data.
key_finding: Main benchmark table (Table 5, 200 scenarios: 40 legitimate / 160 attacks): Distributed Sentinel reaches Precision 0.93 / Recall 0.96 / F1 0.95 / Accuracy 0.92 with 'Low' privacy leakage, versus Prompt Firewall (GPT-5.4 judge) at Precision 1.00 / Recall 0.74 / F1 0.85 / Accuracy 0.79 / 'High' leakage, Rule-Based DLP at Precision 1.00 / Recall 0.48 / F1 0.65 / Accuracy 0.58 / 'High' leakage, and a 'No Protection' baseline at Accuracy 0.20. Per-category recall (Table 6) shows Rule-Based DLP and Prompt Firewall both hit 0% recall on side-channel attacks while Distributed Sentinel achieves 0.95; Rule-Based DLP recall drops to 0.35 on time-series and 0.50 on aggregation/data-reconstruction, where Distributed Sentinel stays at 0.90-0.95. Ablation (Table 7): removing the STT protocol drops F1 from 0.95 to 0.53 (-0.42, the single largest degradation), removing cross-domain query drops it to 0.62 (-0.33), removing neuro-symbolic mapping to 0.79 (-0.16), removing counterfactual simulation to 0.86 (-0.09), removing session provenance to 0.83 (-0.12). Latency (Table 8): end-to-end overhead is ~106ms per agent action, split into 90ms entity extraction (12ms candidate retrieval + 78ms Qwen3-0.6B inference on an A100) and 15.8ms of verification (25% graph fork, 15% STT parsing, 18% local invariant check, 40% cross-sidecar RTT, 2% logging). Scalability (Table 9): F1 stays above 0.92 from 4 to 64 agents while latency grows from 3.2ms to 12.3ms and throughput falls from 2,840 to 1,720 req/s. Multi-agent violation study (Figure 2 / Table 11): unprotected violation rates range from 13.8% (Claude Opus 4.6) to 97.5% (GPT-5.4-mini), with Claude Sonnet 4.6 21.9%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 21.2%, Gemini 2.5 Flash 43.1%, GPT-4.1 40.6%, GPT-5.4 83.8%, GPT-5.4-nano 88.1%; applying Sentinel on top of each model's actual decisions reduces these to a narrow 3.1%-6.2% residual band (Claude Opus 4.6 to 5.6%, Gemini 2.5 Pro to 5.0%, Claude Sonnet 4.6 to 3.8%, GPT-4.1 to 3.1%, Gemini 2.5 Flash to 6.2%, GPT-5.4/GPT-5.4-nano/GPT-5.4-mini all to ~4.4-5.0%), i.e. a stated 59-95% relative reduction across all eight models, and the residual is attributed to entity-resolution errors in the neuro-symbolic mapper rather than STT protocol failure. A separate rule-based PolicyChecker baseline (full access to a structured 82-data-type registry, 17-agent/7-domain mapping, and 15-sink registry) reaches 98.5% agreement with PhantomEcosystem's template-based ground-truth labels (3 disagreements out of 200), which the authors use to validate the benchmark's labels rather than as a competing enforcement system.
core_contribution: The paper (1) formally defines a new multi-agent LLM security threat class called Context-Fragmented Violations (CFVs) -- action sequences where each individual agent action is locally policy-compliant given that agent's private knowledge graph, but the collective sequence violates an organizational invariant because the relevant policy fact (e.g., an NDA-protected project name) lives only in another department's private context; (2) formalizes a companion phenomenon, 'Semantic Laundering', where an LLM transformation (summarization/paraphrase/translation) strips away taint attached to surface text; (3) builds PhantomEcosystem, a 200-scenario benchmark (160 attacks / 40 legitimate controls) across 9 CFV categories, 17 agent types, and 7 organizational domains, with no explicit sensitivity labels in message text and adversarially matched safe controls; (4) proposes Distributed Sentinel, a sidecar/service-mesh-style zero-trust enforcement architecture built around a Semantic Taint Token (STT) protocol -- taints attach to data provenance (source graph node IDs) rather than surface text, are propagated via lightweight encrypted tokens, and are checked via cross-domain predicate queries that return only a boolean (never raw graph data), plus a neuro-symbolic entity-grounding layer (LoRA-fine-tuned Qwen3-0.6B) and pre-execution 'Counterfactual Graph Simulation' (copy-on-write fork + invariant check before commit); and (5) runs an empirical multi-agent violation study of eight frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Flash, GPT-5.4, GPT-5.4-mini, GPT-5.4-nano, GPT-4.1) acting with only per-agent domain world models, showing all models violate at rates from 13.8% to 97.5%, and that applying Distributed Sentinel on top of each model's actual decisions reduces violations by 59-95% across all eight models.
threat_model: Explicitly NOT an adversarial/compromised-agent threat model. The paper's own 'Limitations and Threat Model Scope' section states: 'Our threat model explicitly assumes that agents are not adversarially compromised; defending against prompt injection, jailbreaking, or Byzantine agents is an orthogonal concern.' The threat is honest, non-malicious agents that each act reasonably on their own local (siloed) knowledge graph but produce a collectively policy-violating outcome because critical policy facts (e.g., 'Project Titan is under NDA') are known only to the originating department's agent, not the executing agent. Two attacker-adjacent extensions are only briefly touched: (a) STT signing-key compromise, for which HSMs/key rotation are suggested as mitigations, and (b) a malicious querying sidecar performing repeated adaptive cross-domain queries to reconstruct higher-order graph structure from a series of 1-bit boolean responses ('intersection attacks'), which the authors flag as future work, not something they defend against in the current design.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) threat model excludes adversarially compromised/malicious agents -- prompt injection, jailbreaking, and Byzantine behavior are explicitly out of scope, so Distributed Sentinel is not evaluated against an adaptive attacker trying to evade the sidecar; (2) if a department's STT signing key is compromised, an attacker can forge tokens (mitigation only sketched: HSMs, key rotation); (3) the neuro-symbolic entity mapper is a 'potential single point of failure' and the system's formal soundness guarantee (Theorem 4.1) is conditioned on accurate grounding from free text to graph entities -- in practice this mapper's residual error is the stated cause of the 3.1-6.2% violations that survive Sentinel enforcement; (4) privacy analysis covers only single-query disclosure (<=1 bit per query); the paper explicitly notes that repeated adaptive querying by a malicious sidecar could leak higher-order graph structure via intersection attacks, deferred to future work (rate limiting, differential privacy, adaptive-adversary analysis); (5) PhantomEcosystem's 200 scenarios across 9 categories are acknowledged as possibly not covering all real-world violation patterns, especially domain-specific regulatory requirements or novel attack vectors; (6) the completeness/soundness theorems require every security-relevant constraint to be codified in some department's local graph -- constraints existing only in unstructured documents or tacit human knowledge fall outside the model. Additional grounding notes not flagged by the authors as limitations but relevant to interpreting the numbers: Prompt Firewall, one of the two main baselines, is itself instantiated using GPT-5.4 as judge, and precision in Table 5 is stated to be computed only over methods that produce at least one positive prediction; the ZKP/homomorphic/TEE privacy-enhancement variants (Section 4.8) are architecturally described with isolated micro-benchmarks (Table 3, Table 4) but are not run through the main PhantomEcosystem F1 evaluation, so their effect on detection quality is untested; code and the benchmark dataset are stated as 'to be released upon publication', i.e. not yet available for independent verification at the time of this text.

datasets_used: PhantomEcosystem (introduced in this paper): 200 scenarios total, 160 attack cases / 40 legitimate controls, 9 violation categories (Direct Leak, Multi-hop, Aggregation, Time-series, Side-channel, Scope creep, Data reconstruction, Cross-org, Token manipulation), 17 agent types, 7 organizational domains, 38 data types, average communication chain length 2.4, difficulty split 62 easy / 84 medium / 54 hard
quantitative_results: Main benchmark (Table 5): Distributed Sentinel Precision 0.93 / Recall 0.96 / F1 0.95 / Accuracy 0.92 (Low privacy leakage)
Prompt Firewall (GPT-5.4 judge): Precision 1.00 / Recall 0.74 / F1 0.85 / Accuracy 0.79 (High leakage)
Rule-Based DLP: Precision 1.00 / Recall 0.48 / F1 0.65 / Accuracy 0.58 (High leakage)
Per-category recall (Table 6), overall: Ours 0.96, Prompt Firewall 0.74, Rule-DLP 0.48; both baselines hit 0.00 recall on Side-channel vs. Ours 0.95

Ablation (Table 7): Full system F1 0.95; -STT Protocol F1 0.53 (delta -0.42); -Cross-Domain Query F1 0.62 (-0.33); -Neuro-Symbolic Mapping F1 0.79 (-0.16); -Counterfactual Simulation F1 0.86 (-0.09); -Session Provenance F1 0.83 (-0.12)

Latency (Table 8): total verification 15.8ms (Local Graph Fork 4.0ms/25%, STT Parsing 2.4ms/15%, Local Invariant Check 2.8ms/18%, Cross-Sidecar Query 6.3ms/40%, Decision/Logging 0.3ms/2%); entity extraction ~90ms (12ms retrieval + 78ms Qwen3-0.6B inference); end-to-end ~106ms per agent action
Scalability (Table 9): 4 agents F1 0.95 / 3.2ms / 2,840 req/s; 64 agents F1 0.92 / 12.3ms / 1,720 req/s

User study (Table 10, n=24): agreement with true positives 4.7/5.0, true negatives 4.8/5.0, edge cases 3.9/5.0, blocking-reason clarity 4.3/5.0, perceived workflow disruption 2.1/5.0 (low=good), 92% would recommend deployment

Multi-agent violation study without Sentinel (Table 11 / Figure 2): Claude Opus 4.6 13.8%, Claude Sonnet 4.6 21.9%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 21.2%, Gemini 2.5 Flash 43.1%, GPT-4.1 40.6%, GPT-5.4 83.8%, GPT-5.4-nano 88.1%, GPT-5.4-mini 97.5%

With Sentinel enforcement (Table 11): Claude Opus 4.6 5.6%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 5.0%, Claude Sonnet 4.6 3.8%, GPT-4.1 3.1%, Gemini 2.5 Flash 6.2%, GPT-5.4 5.0%, GPT-5.4-nano 4.4%, GPT-5.4-mini 5.0% -- stated as a 59-95% relative reduction across all 8 models
Rule-based PolicyChecker ground-truth-validation baseline: 98.5% agreement with PhantomEcosystem template labels (3 disagreements / 200 cases)
ZKP overhead micro-benchmark (Table 3, not part of main F1 evaluation): Proof Generation 45.2ms / 192 bytes; Verification 2.1ms; Circuit Setup (one-time) 3,200ms
Neuro-symbolic mapper failure analysis: fine-tuned Qwen3-0.6B mapper violation slip-through rate 2.9% vs. 3.7-4.4% for frontier few-shot models
baselines_compared: Prompt Filtering / Prompt Firewall (GPT-5.4 as single-action policy judge, no cross-domain context)
Monolithic Sentinel (single centralized interceptor with a partial merged graph, described in Section 5.1 but not shown with numbers in the main results tables extracted)
LLM-as-Judge (GPT-4, described in Section 5.1 as a baseline category, mentioned alongside Prompt Firewall)
Rule-based PolicyChecker (used as a benchmark ground-truth validator, not a competing runtime enforcement baseline)
